subject: [PATCH 0/2] btrfs: skip COW for written extent buffers

reviews.author: Sun YangKai
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Sun YangKai noted that the code is unfamiliar to them and provided minor inline suggestions for implementation details, but did not raise any major concerns.

Reviewer Sun YangKai suggested changing the type of the 'ret' variable to unsigned int, as it only contains values returned by radix_tree_gang_lookup_tag, which returns an unsigned int. Additionally, the reviewer questioned the name 'ret', suggesting that it is not a good choice since the function does not actually return this value.

Reviewer Sun YangKai suggested a minor optimization to the code, specifically changing the line where the index is calculated from `index = ret - 1;` to `index=btrfs_root_id(gang[ret-1]) + 1;`, assuming that `ret > ARRAY_SIZE(gang)` should never happen.

Reviewer suggested improving code readability by updating the index variable outside a for loop, making the code look cleaner.
reviews.raw_body:
Thank you for your work on this \u2014 much appreciated.

I am not very familiar with this part of the codebase, so please take my 
comments with that in mind.

That said, I have a few minor inline suggestions regarding some of the 
implementation details. Nothing major \u2014 just small things I noticed 
while reading through. Hope they help. Please see below.

On 2026/4/24 06:43, Leo Martins wrote:

---

I wonder if it would be better to use unsigned int for ret since it only 
set by radix_tree_gang_lookup_tag which returns unsigned int.

And I wonder if ret is a good name since we never return ret.

---

I assume `ret > ARRAAY_SIZE(gang)` should never happen here so maybe we 
could have `index=btrfs_root_id(gang[ret-1]) + 1;` here.

---

index seems not used inside this for loop, so maybe we can update it 
outside the loop to make it looks better.

Thanks again for the patch.
Regards,
Sun YangKai

2026-04-24.patch_summary: This patch series optimizes Btrfs by skipping redundant copy-on-write (COW) operations on extent buffers that have already been written back to disk due to memory pressure. The optimization relies on the WRITTEN flag set by writeback, which indicates that a buffer has been committed to disk and can be safely overwritten in place. This reduces COW amplification, which can exhaust block reservations and degrade throughput under memory pressure. The patches also fix a latent issue in qgroup snapshot accounting, where root items were being persisted mid-transaction, leading to incorrect state and potential corruption. The skip-COW optimization is applied when should_cow_block() encounters a WRITTEN buffer from the current transaction, re-dirtying the buffer instead of allocating a new block.
